## Referee report

### Overall verdict

`answer.tex` is **not answer-ready**. The LaTeX contract appears satisfied, but the mathematical proof has a fatal gap: the central Proposition “Opposition for modular valuated quotients” is essentially asserted rather than proved. In particular, the claimed invariance of all tropical Plücker and incidence-Plücker relations under the flat-lattice anti-involution is not established, and one explanatory statement about the resulting “flat Plücker relations” is actually false as written.

### LaTeX contract check

I recreated and compiled `answer.tex` with `pdflatex`. It compiled successfully to a 4-page PDF. The document class is exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`, `fullpage` is the only layout package, and I did not see forbidden line-spacing, margin, or in-document font-size commands. So I do **not** see a First Proof LaTeX contract violation.

### Literature check

The cited Brandt–Eur–Zhang paper is real and does establish the relevant background correspondence between valuated flag matroids / flag Dressians / flags of projective tropical linear spaces. In particular, their Theorem 4.3.1 states that valuated matroid quotients are equivalent to inclusions of the corresponding projective tropical linear spaces. ([ar5iv.org](https://ar5iv.org/pdf/2005.13727)) The Dress–Wenzel bibliographic citation is also real and is the standard source introducing valuated matroids. ([sciencedirect.com](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/000187089290028J))

However, I did **not** find that the specific “Opposition for modular valuated quotients” proposition in `answer.tex` is a cited theorem from these references. The proof must therefore supply the details, and it does not.

---

## Paragraph-by-paragraph / section-by-section audit

### Conventions

The valuated-matroid model and quotient-order convention are standard and are broadly supported by Brandt–Eur–Zhang’s quotient/inclusion theorem. ([ar5iv.org](https://ar5iv.org/pdf/2005.13727)) But `answer.tex` defines the map on valuated matroid representatives while the problem defines `Dr(M)` as a poset of tropical linear subspaces. To make this well-defined, one needs either to define `Dr(M)` as valuated matroids modulo scalar or to cite/prove that the tropical linear space determines the valuated matroid up to scalar. This uniqueness fact exists in the literature, but it is not mentioned in the solution. ([pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12031988/))

The treatment of loops and projective boundary strata is also not fully specified. Brandt–Eur–Zhang explicitly introduce projective tropical linear spaces to handle loops consistently; the final remark of `answer.tex` only says loops “may be deleted,” without proving that this preserves the relative Dressian and the required involution. ([ar5iv.org](https://ar5iv.org/pdf/2005.13727))

### Section 1: “A consequence of the hypothesis”

The lemma that a finite geometric lattice with an order-reversing involution is modular is essentially correct. Flat lattices of matroids are geometric/semimodular, and the rank inequality used is standard. ([macaulay2.com](https://macaulay2.com/doc/OLD/Macaulay2-1.20/share/doc/Macaulay2/Matroids/html/_lattice__Of__Flats.html))

But the proof omits a needed justification: before “applying the order-reversing involution” to the rank inequality, one must prove that the anti-automorphism sends rank \(i\) to rank \(r-i\). This is true in a finite ranked lattice because maximal chains are reversed, but it should be stated. This is a repairable gap, not the fatal one.

### Section 2: Proposition “Opposition for modular valuated quotients”

This is the critical part of the paper, and it is not rigorously proved.

The first paragraph, proving that a quotient coordinate \(\nu(B)\) depends only on the flat \(\operatorname{cl}_M(B)\), is plausible and probably correct, assuming the precise incidence-Plücker relation being used. The argument should nevertheless explicitly state the relation and handle the edge cases \(k=0\), \(k=r\), and \(\infty\)-valued coordinates.

The fatal problem begins with:

> “Now rewrite all tropical Plucker and incidence-Plucker relations for \(\nu\) in these flat coordinates…”

No explicit list of these “flat Plücker relations” is given. The proof then says they are “intrinsic to the lattice” and supported on “residues,” but these terms are not defined sufficiently to verify the claim. The argument never shows, relation by relation, that applying \(X\mapsto X^\perp\) transforms the usual valuated matroid and quotient relations into the corresponding relations of the complementary rank.

There is also a concrete false statement in this paragraph. The text says:

> “For instance, when \(k=1\) they say exactly that on each rank-two flat of \(M\) the minimum of the point-coordinates is attained at least twice.”

This is false as written. Take \(M=U_{2,4}\), whose lattice is modular and self-dual. There is one rank-two flat, namely \(E\). The vector
\[
x=(0,0,1,1)
\]
has its minimum on \(E\) attained twice. But \(x\) is **not** a rank-one valuated quotient of \(U_{2,4}\), because the actual incidence relation for the triple \(\{1,3,4\}\) requires the minimum of \((0,1,1)\) to be attained at least twice, and it is attained uniquely. I verified this directly by a small code check. Thus the advertised “flat-coordinate” description is at least misstated, and the proof cannot rely on it as written.

This example does not disprove the theorem, but it does show that the proof’s description of the relevant relations is inaccurate. The real relations retain information about subsets/circuits inside a flat, not merely the minimum over the whole flat.

The sentence

> “Consequently the transformed list of tropical equations is precisely the flat Plucker and incidence-Plucker list…”

is the main unproved assertion of the paper. It is exactly the essential lemma needed for the construction, and it is neither proved nor cited.

The subsequent sentence

> “The same residue-by-residue check applied to a three-step flag…”

is also only a placeholder. Order reversal of quotients depends entirely on this claim, but no actual three-rank incidence relation is written down or checked.

### Section 3: Construction of \(\Phi\)

The definition
\[
\Phi(\operatorname{Trop}(\nu))=\operatorname{Trop}(\nu^\perp_M)
\]
depends on the unproved Proposition. It also needs a separate well-definedness argument on tropical linear spaces rather than representatives.

The flat-lattice extension check is mostly plausible under a loopless/simple or carefully projective interpretation. The modular equivalence
\[
Y^\perp\vee F=\hat 1
\iff
Y^\perp\wedge F=\hat 0
\iff
Y\vee F^\perp=\hat 1
\]
is correct given complementary ranks. But the proof should be more explicit about contraction bases, loops in \(F\), and the ground set of \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\).

### The final theorem statement

The final displayed formula is incomplete:
\[
\nu^\perp_M(A)=\nu(B),\qquad
\operatorname{cl}(B)=(\operatorname{cl}(A))^\perp,\quad |A|=r-k .
\]
It omits the necessary case distinction for dependent \(A\). If \(A\) is dependent, \((\operatorname{cl} A)^\perp\) has rank larger than \(k\), so the displayed condition on \(B\) is generally impossible or ambiguous. The earlier proposition includes an \(\infty\) case, but the theorem statement does not.

### Final remark on parallel elements and loops

The remark is not a proof. Parallel elements may be manageable because the incidence relations can force equality inside parallel classes, but this should be integrated into the proof. The statement that loops “may be deleted” is especially underjustified: deleting loops changes the ambient tropical projective space/boundary strata, and the problem did not assume loopless matroids.

---

## Required repairs before answer-readiness

To become rigorous, `answer.tex` would need at least:

1. A precise definition of the “flat Plücker” and “flat incidence-Plücker” relations.
2. A full proof that the order-reversing involution of the modular flat lattice sends those relations of rank \(k\) quotients to the corresponding relations of rank \(r-k\) quotients.
3. A full proof of quotient-order reversal for three-step flags.
4. A proof or citation that the construction is well-defined on tropical linear spaces, not just valuated matroid representatives.
5. A correct handling of dependent subsets in the theorem formula.
6. A rigorous treatment or explicit exclusion of loops and parallel elements.

Because the main proposition is not proved and contains a demonstrably false explanatory characterization, the solution is not complete.

## Overall verdict

The revision is substantially more honest and mathematically safer than the previous draft, but it is **not a complete solution**. It explicitly reduces the problem to an unproved “Flat-coordinate duality lemma” and has a “Remaining open issues” section. Under the stated grading rule, that alone makes the draft not answer-ready.

## LaTeX contract check

I recreated and compiled the revised `answer.tex` with `pdflatex`. It compiled successfully to a 4-page PDF. I found no forbidden `geometry`, manual margin changes, line-spacing changes, or in-document font-size changes. The document class is exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}` and `fullpage` is permitted. So I see **no LaTeX contract violation**.

## Literature validation

The Brandt–Eur–Zhang citation is real, and the cited convention is consistent with their flag Dressian/projective tropical linear space correspondence; search results for their Theorem 4.3.1 specifically state the equivalence between valuated matroid quotients and inclusions of projective tropical linear spaces. ([sciencedirect.com](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S000187082100133X?utm_source=openai))

The Dress–Wenzel citation is also real and is the standard 1992 source for valuated matroids. ([sciencedirect.com](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/000187089290028J?utm_source=openai))

The added Hirai reference is real and does concern a lattice-theoretic characterization of valuated matroids/uniform semimodular lattices, but the abstracted result visible from the source is not the missing flat-coordinate opposition lemma for arbitrary valuated quotient flags inside a modular matroid. ([sciencedirect.com](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0097316519300287))

The draft’s uniqueness claim — that a projective tropical linear space determines its valuated matroid up to common scalar — is standard, and I found a recent source explicitly stating this. However, `answer.tex` itself still gives no citation for that claim. ([pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12031988/?utm_source=openai))

## What the revision fixed from the previous draft

The revision addresses several of the earlier objections:

1. **It no longer claims a complete affirmative proof.** The author now explicitly says the document is a reduction, not a finished answer.

2. **The false rank-two-flat characterization was removed.** The revised draft correctly notes that for \(U_{2,4}\), rank-one quotient conditions are circuit/subset-indexed, not merely “minimum twice on the unique rank-two flat.” I rechecked the example computationally: \(x=(0,0,1,1)\) fails on circuits \(\{1,3,4\}\) and \(\{2,3,4\}\).

3. **The modularity lemma is now better justified.** The revised proof explicitly proves rank reversal under the anti-automorphism before applying semimodularity.

4. **The flat-constancy lemma is now mostly rigorous.** The relevant incidence relation is written down and the comparison of adjacent bases is now substantially clearer.

5. **The dependent-subset case in the candidate formula is fixed.** The previous theorem statement omitted this; the revised candidate correctly assigns \(\infty\) to dependent subsets.

6. **The central missing claim is now isolated.** The “Flat-coordinate duality lemma” is precisely the part that the earlier draft handwaved.

## Remaining mathematical issues

### 1. The main theorem is still unproved

The draft’s Lemma 3, the “Flat-coordinate duality lemma,” is the central mathematical assertion needed to construct the desired involution. It is stated without proof. It is not a minor technicality: it is exactly the relation-by-relation invariance of all tropical Plücker and incidence-Plücker relations under the flat-lattice opposition.

Without this lemma, the draft has not proved that \(\nu^{\perp_M}\) is a valuated matroid, has not proved that it is a quotient of \(\mu_M\), and has not proved quotient-order reversal. Therefore the construction of \(\Phi\) remains conditional.

### 2. The draft explicitly contains unresolved “Remaining open issues”

The section titled “Remaining open issues” says:

> “The only essential gap is Lemma \ref{lem:missing}.”

This directly prevents answer-readiness under the instructions. The problem asks for an actual order-reversing involution on \(\Dr(M)\), not merely a candidate construction conditional on an unproved lemma.

### 3. The simplification/loops/parallel-elements reduction is still underproved

The paragraph saying that loops and parallel elements “do not change the issue” is plausible but not proved at the level needed for a complete solution. In particular:

- The statement that \(\Dr(M)\) is canonically obtained from the simplification by adding loop coordinates and repeating parallel coordinates needs a precise proof.
- The behavior of projective boundary coordinates for loops should be handled explicitly.
- The embedded flats \(F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\) should be checked under the simplification identification.
- The flat lattice is not literally “unchanged” after simplification; it is canonically isomorphic. That distinction matters when extending a named involution \(F\mapsto F^\perp\).

This is a smaller issue than Lemma 3, but it would still need repair in a final proof.

### 4. Endpoint cases in the flat-constancy lemma need attention

The proof of flat constancy uses the incidence relation \(\mathcal I_{k,r}(\nu,\mu_M;A,T)\). This works for \(0<k<r\). But as written, the lemma allows \(k=0\) and \(k=r\).

- For \(k=0\), the statement is trivial, but the proof using \(|A|=k-1\) does not apply.
- For \(k=r\), the convention defined \(\mathcal I_{a,b}\) only for \(a<b\), so \(\mathcal I_{r,r}\) is not available. The proof must either exclude this case or prove separately that rank-\(r\) quotients inside \(\mu_M\) behave as needed.

These are repairable, but they are real gaps in the written proof.

### 5. The support assertion for quotients is used but not proved

After the flat-constancy lemma, the draft says:

\[
\nu(A)=\bar\nu(\cl A)\quad\text{for independent }A,\qquad
\nu(A)=\infty\quad\text{for dependent }A.
\]

The second assertion requires justification: if \(\nu\) is a valuated quotient of \(\mu_M\), then any finite basis of \(\nu\) must be independent in \(M\). This is standard for ordinary quotients/strong maps, but the draft should either prove it from the incidence relations or cite the relevant quotient support property.

### 6. The uniqueness of valuated representatives is still uncited in `answer.tex`

Section 4 says that the standard uniqueness of the valuated matroid representing a projective tropical linear space makes \(\Phi\) independent of representative. This is an important well-definedness point. The revised `references.bib` includes Speyer and Hampe, but `answer.tex`’s actual bibliography does not include them, and no citation is given at the point where uniqueness is invoked.

### 7. The “only possible formula” claim is stronger than justified

The draft says the candidate formula is “the only possible formula compatible with the flat orthocomplement.” This is not proved and is potentially misleading. It is the forced formula if one demands a flat-coordinate opposition on all quotient valuated matroids. But the original problem asks for existence of some order-reversing involution extending the flat involution; a priori, such an involution need not be uniquely determined on all of \(\Dr(M)\). This sentence should be weakened or justified.

### 8. The conditional flat-extension check is plausible but incomplete for dependent sets and nonsimple matroids

The verification that \(q_F\) maps to \(q_{F^\perp}\) is mostly correct for independent \(A\) in the simple loopless reduction. But a complete proof should also explicitly state that dependent \(A\) gives \(\infty\) on both sides, and should integrate the nonsimple case rather than relying on the underproved simplification paragraph.

## Final assessment

The revision is a significant improvement as a **research note / reduction**, but it is not a complete rigorous solution of the stated problem. The principal missing lemma is exactly the hard part needed to prove the proposed involution exists. Several smaller endpoint, support, citation, and simplification details would also need repair before a final proof could be accepted.

## Overall verdict

The revised draft is **not answer-ready**. It now claims a complete affirmative proof, but the proof relies on major “standard facts” that are neither proved nor adequately cited. The most serious new problem is the asserted **valuated Higgs factorization / partial flag completion theorem**. This is essential for both proving that \(\nu^{\perp_M}\in\Dr(M)\) and proving order reversal, but the cited literature does not establish it as stated; in fact, a recent source frames closely related completion of valuated quotient flags into elementary quotients as a question rather than a settled theorem. ([sites.lsa.umich.edu](https://sites.lsa.umich.edu/jidongw/wp-content/uploads/sites/1459/2025/09/2412.12059v1.pdf))

The LaTeX contract appears satisfied, but the mathematical proof still has essential gaps.

I recreated `answer.tex` and ran `pdflatex`. After the usual second run to resolve cross-references, it compiled successfully to a 5-page PDF. The document class is exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`, the only layout package is `fullpage`, and I found no forbidden margin, spacing, or font-size changes. ## Literature validation

The Brandt–Eur–Zhang citation is real and does support the quotient/inclusion convention: their Theorem 4.3.1 states that valuated matroid quotients correspond to inclusions of the associated projective tropical linear spaces. ([par.nsf.gov](https://par.nsf.gov/servlets/purl/10391296))

The Dress–Wenzel and Murota citations are real. Murota’s cited paper gives cryptomorphic independent-set axioms for valuated matroids, but this does **not** by itself validate the draft’s much stronger asserted “valuated Higgs-factorization theorem.” ([sciencedirect.com](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0095895696917232?utm_source=openai))

There are results saying adjacent incidence relations imply Plücker relations in certain full uniform flag settings, but the revised draft uses a broader relative/nonuniform/\(\infty\)-support version without a precise theorem statement or citation. ([academic.oup.com](https://academic.oup.com/imrn/article/2023/19/16748/7005645?utm_source=openai))

Most importantly, a recent paper explicitly discusses completion of a quotient of valuated matroids into elementary quotient steps as a problem/question, and notes only special cases such as elementary quotients or certain endpoint cases. This directly undermines the draft’s unqualified claim that every valuated quotient factors into elementary quotients. ([sites.lsa.umich.edu](https://sites.lsa.umich.edu/jidongw/wp-content/uploads/sites/1459/2025/09/2412.12059v1.pdf))

---

## What the revision fixed from the previous version

The revision addresses several earlier concerns:

1. **The remaining-open-issues section was removed** and replaced by an attempted full proof.

2. **The dependent-subset case remains corrected** in the definition of \(\nu^{\perp_M}\).

3. **The modularity lemma is still essentially correct** and now includes rank reversal.

4. **The flat-constancy lemma is improved** and now handles \(k=0,r\) at least in words.

5. **The extension check on embedded flats now explicitly mentions dependent \(A\)** and is mostly correct in the simple loopless case.

6. **The previous “rank-two-flat minimum” false characterization is no longer used.**

However, the new proof strategy introduces new substantial problems.

---

## Major mathematical issues

### 1. The “valuated Higgs-factorization theorem” is unproved and likely not available as stated

The proof says:

\[
p=h_a\preceq h_{a+1}\preceq\cdots\preceq h_b=q
\]

for every valuated quotient \(p\preceq q\), and calls this the “valuated Higgs-factorization theorem.”

This is essential in two places:

- to insert \(\nu\preceq\mu_M\) into a complete flag;
- to factor \(\theta\preceq\nu\preceq\mu_M\) into elementary steps for order reversal.

But no theorem number or precise citation is given, and the cited sources do not visibly contain this assertion in the needed generality. Worse, recent literature treats completion of valuated quotient flags into elementary quotient flags as a nontrivial question, not as a standard theorem. ([sites.lsa.umich.edu](https://sites.lsa.umich.edu/jidongw/wp-content/uploads/sites/1459/2025/09/2412.12059v1.pdf))

This is a fatal gap. Without this factorization, the construction of \(\Phi\) is not proved to preserve \(\Dr(M)\), and order reversal is not proved.

### 2. The “complete-flag adjacent-relation criterion” is also not adequately justified

The draft states that a full sequence \(g_0,\ldots,g_r\) with trivial endpoints is a valuated complete flag iff all adjacent three-term incidence relations hold, without separately checking Plücker relations.

There are related known results, especially for full uniform flag Dressians, but the draft needs a precise theorem covering:

- nonuniform supports,
- \(\infty\)-valued coordinates,
- relative flags inside a fixed \(\mu_M\),
- possible loops/coloops,
- nonempty support conditions for every rank.

As written, the criterion is too broad. For example, if intermediate ranks were allowed to be all-\(\infty\), adjacent relations could be vacuous while the intermediate object is not a valuated matroid. The proof may only need a restricted version, but that restricted version is not stated or cited.

### 3. The proof of Lemma “elementary opposition” has unresolved edge cases

The local lemma is the new core of the argument. It is promising, but not fully rigorous as written.

#### Endpoint problem

The lemma is stated for \(\rk p=d\), \(\rk q=d+1\), and then fixes a set \(A\) of size \(r-d-2\). If \(d=r-1\), this size is \(-1\). This case occurs when applying the lemma to the top adjacent step \(h_{r-1}\preceq h_r=\mu_M\). There may be no rank \(0\)-to-rank \(1\) incidence condition to check after reversal, but the proof must explicitly separate this endpoint. As written, the lemma is not valid for all \(d\) in its stated range.

#### Degenerate \(\delta=2\) cases

In the \(\delta=2\) case, the proof asserts that

\[
L_x=(\cl(Ax))^\perp,\quad L_y=(\cl(Ay))^\perp,\quad L_z=(\cl(Az))^\perp
\]

are rank \(d+1\) flats in \([G,H]\). This is false if, for example, \(x\in\cl(A)\) while \(y,z\) contribute the two rank increments. Then \(L_x=U^\perp=H\), which has rank \(d+2\), not \(d+1\). In that degenerate case the transformed terms may all be \(\infty\), so the lemma might still be repairable, but the written proof is not correct.

#### Lattice assertions need proof

In the \(\delta=3\) case, the proof asserts that \(P_x,P_y,P_z\) are atoms over \(G\) and that

\[
Q_x=P_y\vee P_z,\qquad Q_y=P_x\vee P_z,\qquad Q_z=P_x\vee P_y.
\]

These statements are plausible consequences of modularity and anti-isomorphism of intervals, but the proof does not actually derive them. A strict proof should show the relevant meet/join computations inside the rank-three modular interval.

### 4. Flat constancy still uses an uncited support assertion

The flat-constancy lemma begins:

> “The last assertion is the usual support statement for matroid quotients…”

This is standard, but still an essential input. Since the draft is otherwise trying to be self-contained about quotient relations, it should either prove from the incidence-Plücker relations that finite \(\nu\)-bases are independent in \(M\), or cite a precise proposition.

The \(k=r\) endpoint assertion that a rank-\(r\) quotient of \(\mu_M\) is a scalar translate of \(\mu_M\) is also plausible but not proved.

### 5. The simplification reduction remains underproved

The draft says loops and parallel elements cause no difficulty and that \(\Dr(M)\) is obtained from the simplification by adding loop coordinates and repeating parallel coordinates.

This is plausible but still not a rigorous reduction. A complete proof should explicitly establish:

- how projective tropical linear spaces behave under deleting loops;
- how quotient coordinates pull back along parallel classes;
- that the embedded flat lattice map \(F\mapsto \Trop(M/F\oplus U_{0,F})\) is preserved under the simplification identification;
- that the given flat-lattice involution descends and lifts correctly.

This is not the main fatal gap, but it is still an omitted argument.

### 6. The proof of order reversal depends entirely on the unproved partial-flag factorization

The final order-reversal argument says:

> “Factor the flag \(\theta\preceq\nu\preceq\mu_M\) into elementary steps…”

This is exactly a partial flag completion theorem for valuated matroids. It is not established by Brandt–Eur–Zhang’s quotient/inclusion theorem; that theorem identifies when a given sequence is a valuated flag, but it does not say every partial valuated flag can be completed to a full elementary flag. ([par.nsf.gov](https://par.nsf.gov/servlets/purl/10391296))

Thus the order-reversal proof is not valid unless the missing factorization theorem is supplied.

---

## Section 4: agreement with flats

This section is mostly correct conditional on the preceding construction. The equivalence

\[
Y^\perp\vee F=\hat 1
\iff
Y^\perp\wedge F=\hat 0
\iff
Y\vee F^\perp=\hat 1
\]

uses modularity and complementary ranks correctly. The dependent-set case is now explicitly mentioned. But this section only proves agreement once \(\Phi\) is already known to be a well-defined order-reversing involution, which remains unproved.

---

## Code/interpreter checks

I used the interpreter to compile the LaTeX and to recheck small finite examples related to the earlier \(U_{2,4}\) issue. The old false characterization has indeed been removed. I did not find a small computational counterexample to the proposed local transform in tiny uniform cases, but such checks do not address the missing factorization and full-flag-completion theorem.

---

## Final assessment

The revised draft is an interesting attempt, and the local modular-interval strategy may be useful. But the proof is not complete. The central new inputs — especially valuated Higgs factorization / completion of arbitrary valuated quotient flags into elementary quotients — are essential, unproved, and not validated by the cited references. The local elementary lemma also has edge-case and degeneracy gaps.

Therefore `answer.tex` does **not** yet fully solve the stated problem as a complete rigorous solution.

## Executive verdict

`answer.tex` is **not a complete solution** to the stated problem. The decisive issue is not hidden: the draft ends with a “Remaining open issues” section stating that the full order-reversal property for arbitrary inclusions in \(\Dr(M)\) has not been proved. Since the question asks for an order-reversing involution on the whole poset \(\Dr(M)\), proving only a set-level involution plus order reversal for elementary/refinable inclusions is insufficient.

## LaTeX contract audit

I reconstructed `answer.tex` and ran `pdflatex` twice. It compiled successfully and produced a 5-page PDF. The source uses exactly `\documentclass[12pt]{article}`, uses the permitted `fullpage` package, and I found no prohibited `geometry`, manual margin changes, line-spacing changes, or in-document font-size changes. Thus I do **not** see a First Proof LaTeX contract violation.

## Literature/source validation

Several cited background facts are real and relevant, but some are used more broadly than the draft justifies.

- Brandt--Eur--Zhang do support the quotient/inclusion framework: their Proposition 4.2.3 identifies flag Dressian points with valuated matroid quotients, and Theorem 4.3.1 states that a valuated matroid quotient is equivalent to inclusion of the associated projective tropical linear spaces. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2005.13727))
- However, BEZ’s tropical incidence-Plücker relations are indexed by arbitrary \(I_0,J_0\), not only nested \(S\subseteq T\). The draft’s formula (1) restricts to \(S\subseteq T\) without proving that this restricted family is equivalent in the non-adjacent rank case. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2005.13727))
- Joswig--Loho--Luber--Olarte Theorem 3.10 indeed says that, in the finite uniform adjacent-rank setting, 3-term tropical incidence relations force the two functions to be valuated matroids. But the theorem as stated there is for real-valued functions on uniform layers, whereas the draft applies analogous statements in nonuniform and \(\infty\)-valued relative settings. ([academic.oup.com](https://academic.oup.com/imrn/article/2023/19/16748/7005645))
- Jarra--Lorscheid provide relevant flag-matroid-over-tract results, including a theorem relating adjacent quotients and flags over perfect tracts, and a proposition comparing valuated flag matroids with tropical-hyperfield flag matroids. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2204.04658))
- The Murota 1997 paper is correctly identified as concerning cryptomorphic axioms for valuated matroids on independent sets, but the draft’s specific use of “Theorem 2.1” for the exact truncation/relative Higgs elongation construction is not verified in the text and is doing essential work. ([sciencedirect.com](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0095895696917232?utm_source=openai))

## Paragraph-by-paragraph mathematical audit

### Conventions and standard inputs

The shift from “\(\Dr(M)\) as tropical linear subspaces” to “\(\Dr(M)\) as valuated matroid quotients \(\nu\preceq\mu_M\)” is standard but should be stated more carefully. The problem defines \(\Dr(M)\) as a set of tropical linear spaces, so a complete proof must ensure that the proposed formula is well-defined on tropical linear spaces, not merely on chosen valuated Plücker representatives.

The incidence relation (1) is a serious point. The draft states that quotient relations of arbitrary ranks \(a\le b\) are characterized by
\[
\min_{i\in T\setminus S}\{p(Si)+q(T-i)\}
\]
for \(S\subseteq T\). BEZ’s incidence-Plücker relations are more general. If the author wants to use only the nested relations, they must prove or cite a precise theorem saying that, together with the valuated matroid axioms, the nested relations suffice for arbitrary rank gaps. Without that, the “full incidence” discussion in the Remaining open issues section may not even be the full BEZ incidence condition.

The “complete sequence iff all adjacent relations (2) hold” input is also essential. It is plausible in the intended tropical-hyperfield/perfect-tract framework, but the draft cites several sources without spelling out the exact theorem, hypotheses, and sign/min conventions. A strict standalone proof should state a precise lemma covering nonuniform supports and \(\infty\)-coordinates.

The insertion formula (3) is another major unproved input. The proposition later relies on it to insert every quotient \(\nu\preceq\mu_M\) into a complete flag ending at \(\mu_M\). In particular, the claim \(h_r=\mu_M\) after scalar normalization is nontrivial: it requires showing that
\[
B\mapsto \min\{\nu(C):C\subseteq B,\ |C|=k\}
\]
is constant over all bases \(B\) of \(M\). The draft gives no proof and no precise theorem statement with hypotheses.

### Lemma 1: modularity of the flat lattice

This lemma is essentially correct. A finite ranked semimodular lattice with an order-reversing anti-automorphism is both semimodular and lower semimodular, hence modular. The rank-reversal argument is acceptable, though it would be cleaner to explicitly note that the involution sends \(\hat0\) to \(\hat1\) and intervals anti-isomorphically.

### Lemma 2: flat constancy

The proof is mostly sound. The use of the basis graph of a restriction to a rank \(k\) flat is standard, and the incidence relation with \(S=A\), \(T=A\cup\{b,b'\}\cup C\) correctly forces \(\nu(Ab)=\nu(Ab')\), assuming the incidence relation being used is valid for \(\nu\preceq\mu_M\).

Minor issues:
- The support assertion relies on the fact that the underlying matroid of a valuated quotient of \(\mu_M\) is an ordinary quotient of \(M\); this should be cited or proved.
- The proof uses the convention that a tropical minimum of all \(\infty\) terms is “attained at least twice.” This is standard but should be stated.
- The notation \(Ab\), \(Abb'\), etc., is clear but informal.

### Section 2: opposition transform

The definition
\[
\nu^{\perp_M}(A)=\bar\nu((\operatorname{cl} A)^\perp)
\]
for independent \(A\) of size \(r-k\) is well-defined using flat constancy and rank reversal. The formula is indeed involutive on flat coordinates.

However, at this point the draft has not yet shown that \(\nu^{\perp_M}\) is a valuated matroid or a quotient of \(\mu_M\). That is deferred to Proposition 3 and depends on the strong complete-flag insertion input.

Also, the draft should explicitly address projective equivalence and well-definedness on tropical linear spaces, since the problem’s \(\Dr(M)\) is phrased as tropical subspaces rather than Plücker vectors.

### Lemma 3: elementary opposition

The local case analysis is the strongest part of the draft. The modular-rank computations in the \(\delta\le1\), \(\delta=2\), and \(\delta=3\) cases are plausible and mostly correct.

Still, several details need tightening:

- In the \(\delta=2\) case, the statement “the finite terms already contain two equal flats” is not by itself enough unless one also proves that any remaining finite term cannot have strictly smaller value. In the intended rank-two configuration this is probably true because the third term is forced to be \(\infty\), but the proof should say so explicitly.
- The phrase “has precisely the three finite terms \(\bar q(L_x),\bar q(L_y),\bar q(L_z)\)” is inaccurate if some of the \(\bar q(L_e)\) are \(\infty\). What is true is that these are the only terms not forced to be \(\infty\) by the \(\mu_M\)-coordinate.
- The lemma proves only adjacent-rank incidence relations for the transformed pair. It does not prove that the transform carries arbitrary quotient relations to quotient relations.

These are repairable local-writing issues, not the main fatal gap.

### Proposition 3: set involution and elementary order reversal

This proposition is conditional on the earlier unproved “standard input” that every single quotient \(\nu\preceq\mu_M\) can be inserted into a complete flag using formula (3). If that input is accepted, the argument that \(\nu^{\perp_M}\preceq\mu_M\) is plausible.

The proposition proves:
1. a set-level involution on represented valuated quotients;
2. order reversal for elementary inclusions;
3. order reversal for inclusions admitting an elementary refinement.

It does **not** prove order reversal for arbitrary inclusions in \(\Dr(M)\). Since the problem asks for an order-reversing involution on the entire poset, this is insufficient.

### Section 4: agreement with the flat involution

This section is largely correct, conditional on Proposition 3. The equivalence
\[
q_F(B)<\infty \Longleftrightarrow \operatorname{cl}(B)\vee F=\hat1
\]
is the right basis condition for \(M/F\oplus U_{0,F}\), and modularity correctly converts
\[
Y^\perp\vee F=\hat1
\]
to
\[
Y\vee F^\perp=\hat1.
\]
Thus the candidate map agrees with the flat involution on the embedded copy of \(\mathcal L(M)^{\mathrm{op}}\).

Minor issue: the map \(\Phi\) is used here without having been formally named as \(\nu\mapsto\nu^{\perp_M}\), though the intent is clear.

### Remaining open issues

This section is fatal for readiness. It explicitly says that the draft has not proved that every inclusion in the relative Dressian admits an elementary refinement, nor that the full incidence relations are carried to the full transformed incidence relations. That missing step is exactly what is needed to prove that the candidate \(\Phi\) is order-reversing on the whole poset.

Also, the word “Equivalently” is too strong. A relative partial-flag completion theorem and a direct full-incidence duality theorem would be alternative sufficient routes for this candidate construction, but they are not literally equivalent as stated. Moreover, the original problem asks for existence of some order-reversing involution, not necessarily this specific flat-coordinate transform.

## Final assessment

The draft contains meaningful partial progress, especially the modular-lattice reduction, flat constancy lemma, local elementary-opposition calculation, and verification on embedded flats. But it does not solve the stated problem. The main order-reversal property for arbitrary inclusions remains unproved, and several essential standard inputs are invoked without precise hypotheses.

Therefore `answer.tex` is **not answer-ready**.

## Verdict

The revision is a substantial improvement: it removes the explicit “Remaining open issues” section and adds a direct full-incidence-duality argument. However, I still do **not** regard `answer.tex` as a complete rigorous solution. The new proof depends on several essential “standard” inputs that are either stated in a form stronger/different from the cited literature or are not proved/cited precisely enough. The most serious unresolved issue is the use of a **nested incidence criterion** as if it were the full valuated quotient criterion; the literature I checked formulates the relevant incidence-Plücker relations with arbitrary index sets, and the draft does not prove the claimed equivalence.

The cited BEZ paper does support the general framework that valuated matroid quotients correspond to inclusions of projective tropical linear spaces: Proposition 4.2.3 identifies flag Dressian points with valuated matroid quotients, and Theorem 4.3.1 states \(\mu\preceq\nu\) iff \(\operatorname{trop}(\mu)\subseteq\operatorname{trop}(\nu)\). But BEZ’s incidence-Plücker conditions are indexed by arbitrary \(I'\in\binom{[n]}{r-1}\) and \(J'\in\binom{[n]}{s+1}\), not only by nested \(S\subseteq T\). ([par.nsf.gov](https://par.nsf.gov/servlets/purl/10391296))

Jarra--Lorscheid support the broader flag-matroid-over-tract framework and the equivalence between quotient chains and flags over perfect tracts, but their theorem applies once one already has quotients in the appropriate sense; it does not by itself justify replacing arbitrary incidence relations by the nested subset used in this draft. ([sciencedirect.com](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S000187082300539X?utm_source=openai))

Joswig--Loho--Luber--Olarte verify that in the **finite uniform adjacent-rank** setting, 3-term tropical incidence relations force the two functions to be valuated matroids. This is relevant background, but it does not directly cover the nonuniform, \(\infty\)-valued, relative-\(M\) complete-flag criterion used in Proposition 2. ([academic.oup.com](https://academic.oup.com/imrn/article/2023/19/16748/7005645))

Murota’s paper exists and is correctly about cryptomorphic axioms for valuated matroids on independent sets, but the revised draft still does not give a precise theorem number or statement covering the exact relative truncation/elongation completion used in formula (1). ([sciencedirect.com](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0095895696917232?utm_source=openai))

## What the revision addresses

The revision does address several previous concerns:

1. **The explicit open gap was removed.** The new Section 3 attempts a direct proof of full order reversal for arbitrary rank gaps.
2. **The endpoint \(h_r=\mu_M\) in the completion construction is now checked.** The basis-graph argument is plausible, modulo small edge cases.
3. **The local elementary opposition argument was slightly clarified.**
4. **An ordinary-support check was added.** This is the new “ordinary polar quotients” lemma.
5. **The flat-agreement section is still essentially correct**, assuming the preceding construction is valid.

However, the revision also introduces or exposes new essential gaps.

## Major remaining issues

### 1. The nested incidence criterion is asserted, not proved

The proof’s central order-reversal theorem checks only relations of the form
\[
\min_{i\in T\setminus S}\{p(S\cup i)+q(T\setminus i)\}
\]
with \(S\subseteq T\). The draft states that these nested relations, together with valuated-matroid axioms and the ordinary quotient condition, are equivalent to the usual arbitrary-index incidence-Plücker relations.

This equivalence is not proved and is not precisely cited. It is not a harmless convention: BEZ’s quotient/inclusion theorem uses arbitrary \(I'\), \(J'\). Therefore, the new Theorem 3 proves at most order reversal for the draft’s **asserted nested quotient criterion**, not for the quotient relation known to characterize tropical linear-space inclusion, unless the missing equivalence is supplied.

This is a fatal gap.

### 2. The complete-flag cryptomorphism is still too imprecise

Proposition 2 uses the following chain of reasoning:

- insert \(\nu\) into a complete flag via formula (1);
- apply the elementary opposition lemma to adjacent pairs;
- conclude that the reversed sequence is a complete valuated flag because it satisfies adjacent incidence relations.

But the draft does not provide a precise theorem proving that, in this nonuniform relative setting with \(\infty\)-values and prescribed top endpoint \(\mu_M\), adjacent transformed three-term relations alone imply that every layer is a valuated matroid and every adjacent pair is a quotient. The cited uniform result of Joswig--Loho--Luber--Olarte is not enough by itself, and the Murota citation is not stated precisely enough to cover this exact claim.

This remains an essential unproved input.

### 3. The one-point completion formula is still underjustified

The added proof that \(h_r=\mu_M\) is useful, but the full completion claim requires much more:

- each \(h_j\) must be a valuated matroid;
- the supports must be correct relative to \(M\);
- adjacent quotient relations \(h_j\preceq h_{j+1}\) must hold;
- the construction must handle \(\infty\)-values and nonuniform supports.

The draft says this is “Murota’s valuated truncation/elongation” but does not state a precise theorem with hypotheses matching this use. Since Proposition 2, and hence the entire construction of \(\nu^{\perp_M}\) as an element of \(\Dr(M)\), depends on this, the citation is not currently adequate for a complete proof.

### 4. The new “ordinary polar quotients” lemma is essentially unproved

Lemma `ordinary polar quotients` is new and crucial. It asserts the rank formula
\[
\rho_{N^{\perp_M}}(X)=\rk_M(X)-k+\rho_N(X^\perp)
\]
for the support of the polar transform, then uses it to prove the required ordinary quotient order.

The proof is only:
> “Formula (3) is the usual polar-dual rank formula for matroids on a modular geometric lattice…”

This is not a proof and is not tied to a precise citation. Several details need justification:

- that the support defined by (2) is indeed the set of bases of a matroid with this rank function;
- that the formula is valid for nonsimple matroids with loops/parallel classes, not only for projective geometries or \(q\)-matroids;
- that checking rank increments only on flats of \(M\) suffices for the ordinary strong-map criterion on all subsets.

Because this lemma supplies the ordinary quotient condition needed in Theorem 3, this is another essential gap.

## Major remaining issue: Lemma `localflag`

The whole proof depends on Lemma `localflag`, which states three strong facts:

1. Nested incidence relations imply arbitrary BEZ incidence relations, assuming valuatedness and ordinary support quotient.
2. A complete sequence with endpoints \(g_0\) and \(\mu_M\) is a complete valuated flag iff adjacent nested three-term relations hold.
3. Formula (1) inserts any quotient \(\nu\preceq\mu_M\) into such a complete flag.

This lemma is doing a large amount of essential work. The proof is still too compressed to be acceptable as a rigorous standalone argument.

### Specific problems with Lemma `localflag`

- It invokes a “Murota--Shioura local-to-global theorem” but does not state the theorem precisely, give a theorem number, or verify the hypotheses. In particular, the proof must check that the relevant two-layer or multi-layer flag support is an appropriate generalized polymatroid / \(M^\natural\)-convex domain.

- It does not carefully handle \(\infty\)-valued functions and nonuniform supports. The available adjacent-rank incidence-implies-Plücker result I found in the literature is explicitly finite and uniform. ([academic.oup.com](https://academic.oup.com/imrn/article/2023/19/16748/7005645))

- The phrase “after removing the common part the local exchange has exactly the form \(S\subseteq T\)” is not enough. For arbitrary incidence \(I,J\), the non-nested case corresponds to basis pairs with more than one element on the smaller-side difference. The draft needs a detailed derivation from the local exchange axiom to all arbitrary incidence relations.

- The complete-flag part says adjacent nested three-term relations alone force every layer to be a valuated matroid and every adjacent pair to be a quotient. This is a strong incidence-implies-Plücker statement in a nonuniform relative setting. It is not proved in the draft, and the cited literature in the draft does not directly state it in this form.

- Formula (1) is asserted to be Dress--Terhalle/Murota truncation and Higgs elongation. That is plausible, but the draft does not prove that every \(h_j\) is a valuated matroid, that the adjacent quotient relations hold, or that the top endpoint is exactly \(\mu_M\) up to scalar in the required relative setting.

Because Proposition `prop:set` and Theorem `thm:order` both depend on Lemma `localflag`, this is a fatal gap for answer-readiness.

## Detailed audit

### Opening conventions

The valuated-matroid interpretation of \(\Dr(M)\) is reasonable and supported by the Brandt–Eur–Zhang quotient/inclusion theorem. However, the problem statement defines \(\Dr(M)\) as a poset of tropical linear subspaces, while the answer works with projective valuated matroids. The answer should explicitly state that it is using the standard identification between projective valuated matroids modulo scalar and their projective tropical linear spaces. As written, this is mostly implicit.

The full incidence-Plücker condition (A) is correctly stated in spirit. It matches the literature’s arbitrary \(I,J\) incidence relations, not just nested ones.

### The “local flag criterion” lemma

This is the most serious problem.

The first assertion says full incidence (A) is equivalent to nested incidence (N), assuming the ordinary supports are in quotient order. This may be true, but `answer.tex` does **not** prove it. The given proof says:

> “For two ranks, the support condition says exactly that the union of the two basis layers is an ordinary flag matroid, i.e. a \(0\)-\(1\) generalized polymatroid.”

This statement is false for non-adjacent ranks. For example, take supports \(U_{2,6}\) and \(U_{4,6}\), which are in ordinary quotient order. The union of the 2-subsets and 4-subsets is not an \(M^\natural\)-convex set / generalized polymatroid: with \(X=\{1,2,3,4\}\), \(Y=\{1,2\}\), and \(i=3\in X\setminus Y\), the \(B^\natural\)-exchange axiom fails because the intermediate 3-subsets are not in the domain and \(Y\setminus X=\varnothing\). Thus Murota–Shioura’s local theorem cannot be applied to the two-layer domain in the way claimed.

Moreover, Murota–Shioura’s local theorem concerns local exchange conditions \(L1,L2,L3\), whereas relation (N) is a nested incidence relation with \(|T\setminus S|=b-a+2\), which is not local when \(b-a>1\). The proof does not explain how to pass from Murota–Shioura’s theorem to exactly (N), nor how (N) implies all arbitrary incidence-Plücker relations (A).

This gap is essential because Theorem 3.2 later proves only nested incidence for \(q^{\perp_M}\preceq p^{\perp_M}\) and then invokes this lemma to conclude the full quotient relation.

The complete-flag part of the lemma is also under-justified. It may be recoverable from \(M^\natural\)-concavity on all ranks or from Jarra–Lorscheid, but the answer does not precisely state which theorem says that the displayed adjacent three-term relations alone imply all Plücker and flag-Plücker relations for every layer, especially with nonuniform supports and \(\infty\)-coordinates.

The insertion formula (1) is likewise cited as Dress–Terhalle–Murota well-layering, but the proof only checks the top layer. It does not prove or cite a precise theorem showing that all \(h_j\) are valuated matroids and form a complete valuated flag.

### Lemma 1.1: modularity of the flat lattice

This argument is essentially correct. A lattice anti-automorphism reverses ranks, and applying it to semimodularity gives the reverse inequality, hence modularity. No serious issue here.

### Lemma 1.2: flat constancy

The proof is mostly sound. The use of the incidence relation with lower set \(A\) and upper set \(A\cup\{b,b'\}\cup C\) correctly forces \(\nu(Ab)=\nu(Ab')\), including the case where both are \(\infty\). Minor point: the proof should explicitly say that \(C\) can be chosen so both \(AbC\) and \(Ab'C\) are bases because \(Ab\) and \(Ab'\) span the same rank-\(k\) flat.

### Definition of \(\nu^{\perp_M}\)

The formula is well-defined if flat constancy holds. The involutivity on flat coordinates is plausible. No major issue here.

### Lemma 2.1: ordinary polar rank

The lemma is important, but the proof is too compressed.

The claim that
\[
\rho^\perp(X)=\rk_M(X)-k+\rho_N(X^\perp)
\]
extends to a matroid rank function requires more verification than given. One must check:

1. \(\rho^\perp\) is constant on \(M\)-closures.
2. \(0\le \rho^\perp(A)\le |A|\) for arbitrary subsets.
3. \(\rho^\perp\) has unit increments.
4. Submodularity holds for arbitrary subsets, not only \(M\)-flats.
5. The use of \(\rho_N(X^\perp\vee Y^\perp)\) is legitimate, since \(\rho_N\) is a set rank function and the join is an \(M\)-closure.

These facts are likely true using the quotient rank-increment inequality, but they are not proved. The final assertion that arbitrary subsets reduce to \(M\)-closures is also not justified. This is a fixable but real gap.

### Lemma 2.2: elementary opposition

The case analysis appears mostly correct, but several details are left implicit:

- In the \(\delta=2\) case, the proof should explicitly identify the transformed three terms
  \[
  q^{\perp_M}(Ax)+p^{\perp_M}(Ayz),\quad
  q^{\perp_M}(Ay)+p^{\perp_M}(Axz),\quad
  q^{\perp_M}(Az)+p^{\perp_M}(Axy).
  \]
- The claims about loops and coincident points in the rank-two interval should be spelled out carefully.
- In the \(\delta=3\) case, the proof should state that \(G=W^\perp\) has rank \(d-1\), so that a basis \(S\) of \(G\) has the right size for the adjacent incidence relation of \(p\preceq q\).

These are mostly clarity issues, not necessarily fatal.

### Proposition 2.3: set-level involution on \(\Dr(M)\)

This proposition depends directly on the local flag criterion and on the unproved completeness of the flag constructed by (1). Since those inputs are not rigorously established, this proposition is not fully proved.

If Lemma 2.2 and a correct complete-flag local-to-global theorem were supplied, the argument would be plausible.

### Lemma 3.1: circuit minima

The idea is correct, but the proof should specify that \(D\) is chosen as a common complement to the flat spanned by \(A\cup C\). As written, “Choose \(D\)” after “For each \(i\in C\)” is ambiguous: the same \(D\) must work for all \(i\in C\) in the single incidence relation.

This is fixable.

### Theorem 3.2: full order reversal

The internal case analysis for nested incidence is strong and appears essentially correct. The \(\delta=m\) Boolean-frame argument and the \(\delta=m-1\) unique-circuit argument are plausible.

However, the theorem only checks nested incidence. The conclusion that this gives the full valuated quotient relation relies entirely on the first part of Lemma 1, whose proof is invalid for non-adjacent ranks. Therefore Theorem 3.2 is not rigorously established.

The edge cases \(a=0\) and \(b=r\) are handled adequately, though the \(b=r\) case should explicitly note that the rank-zero lower transformed valuated matroid is automatically a quotient of the upper one.

### Agreement with the flat involution

This section is essentially correct. The equivalence
\[
Y^\perp\vee F=\hat 1
\iff
Y^\perp\wedge F=\hat 0
\iff
Y\vee F^\perp=\hat 1
\]
uses modularity and complementary ranks correctly. The basis condition for \(M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}\) is correctly identified.

This part would be acceptable once the global construction of \(\Phi\) is made rigorous.

## Previous concerns: what was fixed and what remains

### 1. Nested-to-full incidence criterion

This concern is mostly addressed. The new proof of Criterion 0.1 is substantially better than the previous attempted Murota–Shioura shortcut.

The argument is:

1. Fix \(J\) and set \(c_j=q(J\setminus j)\).
2. If some \(c_j\) is finite, then \(J\) contains a \(q\)-basis, hence spans the support matroid of \(p\).
3. The nested incidence relations with upper set \(J\) say \(c\) is orthogonal to the cocircuits of \(p|J\).
4. Since the tropical hyperplane orthogonal to \(c\) is tropically convex and contains the cocircuits, it contains the tropical linear space of \(p|J\).
5. The restricted cocircuit \(C_p^*(I)|_J\) is a vector of \(p|J\) by the Plücker relations of \(p\).

This is plausible. I would still ask the author to cite the precise cocircuit-span theorem and mention closure of the tropical span if needed, but I do not see a fatal flaw in this criterion.

### 2. Complete-flag local criterion

This remains unresolved in the written proof, despite the new Wang citation. The problem is no longer an omitted reference; it is a **false proposition**.

`answer.tex` states:

> Suppose that for each \(1\le j<r\), each \(S\), and distinct \(x,y,z\), the displayed three-term minimum relation holds. Then every \(w_j\) is a valuated matroid and \(w_0\preceq\cdots\preceq w_r\).

This is not true. Wang’s theorem does not say that only the displayed concurrent minimum relations suffice in complete generality. Wang’s “three-term incidence relations” include degenerate hyperplane constraints and parallel hyperplane constraints, not just the displayed minimum over three terms.

#### Explicit counterexample to Proposition 0.2

Let
\[
E=\{\ell_1,\ell_2,\ell_3,a,b,c,d\}.
\]
Let \(w_4\) be the trivial rank-4 valuated matroid whose only finite basis is \(\{a,b,c,d\}\), so \(\ell_1,\ell_2,\ell_3\) are loops. Define:

- \(w_0(\varnothing)=0\);
- \(w_1\) finite exactly on \(\{a\},\{b\},\{c\},\{d\}\);
- \(w_2\) finite exactly on two-subsets of \(\{a,b,c,d\}\);
- \(w_3\) finite exactly on three-subsets of \(\{a,b,c,d\}\), **and also** on \(\{\ell_1,\ell_2,\ell_3\}\).

All finite values are \(0\), all other values are \(\infty\).

A direct finite check shows that all displayed adjacent three-term minimum relations in Proposition 0.2 hold. The nonloop part is just the ordinary uniform flag, and any term involving the extra finite coordinate \(w_3(\ell_1\ell_2\ell_3)\) is paired with an \(\infty\)-term from \(w_2\) or \(w_4\), so it never creates a unique finite minimum.

But \(w_3\) is not a valuated matroid: its finite support is not the set of bases of a matroid. Basis exchange fails between
\[
\{\ell_1,\ell_2,\ell_3\}
\quad\text{and}\quad
\{a,b,c\}.
\]
For example, removing \(\ell_1\) from the first basis and adding any of \(a,b,c\) does not produce a finite \(w_3\)-basis.

Thus Proposition 0.2 is false as stated.

This is a fatal issue because Proposition 2.4 invokes Proposition 0.2 to conclude that the reversed polar sequence is a complete valuated flag.

A repair may be possible: the author could restate the flag criterion with the full Wang relations, including degenerate and parallel relations, or add precise ordinary-support hypotheses and prove that the polar sequence satisfies all Wang relations. But this is not done in the current `answer.tex`.

\documentclass[12pt]{article}
\usepackage{fullpage}
\usepackage{amsmath,amssymb,amsthm}

\newcommand{\cL}{\mathcal L}

\newcommand{\Trop}{\operatorname{Trop}}

\newcommand{\cl}{\operatorname{cl}}
\newcommand{\op}{\operatorname{op}}

\newtheorem{theorem}{Theorem}
\newtheorem{lemma}{Lemma}
\newtheorem{proposition}{Proposition}
\newtheorem{criterion}{Criterion}

\begin{document}

\begin{center}
{\bf A polar transform for the relative Dressian}
\end{center}

\section*{0. Conventions and two local facts}

Let $E$ be the ground set, $r=\rk M$, and $\mu_M$ the trivial rank $r$
valuated matroid supported on the bases of $M$.  I use the standard
model of the relative Dressian: a rank $k$ point is a projective
valuated matroid $\nu$ with $\nu\preceq\mu_M$, where $\preceq$ denotes
valuated quotient.  Brandt--Eur--Zhang \cite{BEZ} identify this order with
inclusion of projective tropical linear spaces,
\[
        \Trop(\theta)\subseteq\Trop(\nu)
        \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \theta\preceq\nu .
\]
For valuated matroids $p,q$ of ranks $a\le b$, $p\preceq q$ is
equivalent to the incidence-Plucker relations
\[
 \min_{i\in J\setminus I}\{p(I\cup i)+q(J\setminus i)\}
 \quad\hbox{attained at least twice}                         \tag{IP}
\]
for all $I\in\binom{E}{a-1}$ and $J\in\binom{E}{b+1}$.  All-$\infty$
minima are regarded as attained at every displayed index.

\begin{criterion}[nested incidence suffices]\label{crit:local}
Let $p,q$ be valuated matroids of ranks $a<b$ whose ordinary supports
are in quotient order.  If {\rm (IP)} holds whenever $I\subseteq J$,
then {\rm (IP)} holds for all $I,J$.
\end{criterion}

\begin{proof}
For a rank $a$ valuated matroid $p$ and an $(a-1)$-set $I$, write
$C^*_p(I)_e=p(I\cup e)$, with value $\infty$ if $e\in I$.  We use the
standard cocircuit-generation theorem of Dress--Wenzel/Speyer \cite{DW,Speyer}: the
tropical linear space of a valuated matroid is the closed tropical convex
hull of its valuated cocircuits.

Fix arbitrary $I\in\binom{E}{a-1}$ and $J\in\binom{E}{b+1}$, and put
$c_j=q(J\setminus j)$ for $j\in J$.  If all $c_j$ are $\infty$ there is
nothing to prove.  Otherwise $J$ contains a $q$-basis.  Since the
ordinary support of $p$ is a quotient of the ordinary support of $q$,
every $q$-basis spans the support matroid of $p$ (indeed,
$a-\rk_p(Q)\le b-\rk_q(Q)=0$ for a $q$-basis $Q$); hence the
restriction $p|J$ has rank $a$.

For every $S\subseteq J$ with $|S|=a-1$, the nested hypothesis with
upper set $J$ says precisely that the cocircuit $C^*_{p|J}(S)$ is
tropically orthogonal to $c=(c_j)_{j\in J}$.  The set of vectors
orthogonal to a fixed $c$ is a tropical hyperplane, hence is tropically
convex; therefore it contains the whole tropical linear space of $p|J$.
It remains only to note that $C^*_p(I)|_J$ is a vector of $p|J$: for
each valuated circuit of $p|J$, indexed by a set
$D\in\binom{J}{a+1}$, the orthogonality relation
\[
 \min_{e\in D}\{p(I\cup e)+p(D\setminus e)\}
\]
is exactly a Plucker relation for $p$.  Hence $C^*_p(I)|_J$ is
orthogonal to $c$, which is the desired arbitrary incidence relation.
\end{proof}

We also use Wang's codimension-one theorem in the following precise
form.  In Wang's terminology the ``three-term incidence relations''
consist of degenerate, parallel, and concurrent hyperplane relations;
the displayed tropical minimum below is only the concurrent relation.

\begin{proposition}[elementary quotient test]\label{prop:elementarytest}
Let $\eta$ be a rank $d$ valuated matroid and let
$\theta:\binom{E}{d-1}\to{\mathbb R}\cup\{\infty\}$ be nonzero.  Suppose
that
\[
        \theta(A)=\infty\quad\hbox{whenever }A
        \hbox{ is dependent in the support matroid of }\eta              \tag{D}
\]
and, when $d\ge2$, for every $S\in\binom{E}{d-2}$ and distinct $x,y,z\notin S$,
\[
\min\{\theta(Sx)+\eta(Syz),\,
      \theta(Sy)+\eta(Sxz),\,
      \theta(Sz)+\eta(Sxy)\}                                      \tag{C}
\]
is attained at least twice.  Then $\theta$ is a valuated matroid and
$\theta\preceq\eta$.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
For $d=1$ this is the unique rank-zero quotient.  Let $d\ge2$.  By
Wang's Theorem 4.3 \cite{Wang}, $\Dr^1(\eta)$ is cut out by the
degenerate, parallel, and concurrent three-term incidence relations.
Condition (D) is exactly the degenerate part, and (C) contains the
concurrent part.  It remains only to see that the parallel part follows
from (C).  Write $A=Di$ and $B=Dj$ for independent $(d-1)$-sets in the
support matroid of $\eta$ with the same closure, and choose $k$ outside
that closure, so $Dik$ and $Djk$ are bases.  Applying (C) to
$D,i,j,k$, the third upper coordinate $\eta(Dij)$ is $\infty$, while
$\eta(Dik)$ and $\eta(Djk)$ are finite.  Hence
\[
        \theta(Di)+\eta(Djk)=\theta(Dj)+\eta(Dik),
\]
which is Wang's parallel hyperplane relation.  Thus all of Wang's
defining relations hold, so $\theta$ represents a codimension-one
tropical linear subspace of $\Trop(\eta)$.
\end{proof}

Finally, if $\nu\preceq\mu_M$ has rank $k$, we use its valuated Higgs
completion
\[
 h_j(B)=
 \begin{cases}
 \min\{\nu(C): B\subseteq C,\ |C|=k\}, & j\le k,\ |B|=j,\\
 \min\{\nu(C): C\subseteq B,\ |C|=k\}, & j\ge k,\ B\in\mathcal I_j(M),\\
 \infty, & j\ge k,\ B\notin\mathcal I_j(M).
 \end{cases}                                                \tag{1}
\]
Murota's truncation and relative elongation theorems \cite[Theorems 2.1 and 4.1]{Murota}, equivalently the
Dress--Terhalle well-layering construction \cite{DT}, give valuated matroids
$h_j$ and adjacent quotients $h_j\preceq h_{j+1}$.  The top layer is
projectively $\mu_M$: if $B'=B-e+f$ are adjacent $M$-bases and
$C\subseteq B$ minimizes (1), then either $e\notin C$ and $C\subseteq B'$,
or the incidence relation for $\nu\preceq\mu_M$ with lower set $C-e$ and
upper set $B\cup f$ gives a $k$-subset of $B'$ of value at most
$\nu(C)$; by symmetry the two top-layer values are equal.

\section*{1. Flat-lattice duality and flat constancy}

\begin{lemma}\label{lem:modular}
If $\cL(M)$ admits an order-reversing involution $X\mapsto X^\perp$,
then $\cL(M)$ is modular and $\rk(X^\perp)=r-\rk(X)$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The anti-automorphism reverses maximal chains, so it sends rank $i$ to
rank $r-i$.  Applying it to the semimodular inequality for the geometric
lattice $\cL(M)$ gives the reverse inequality, hence equality.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[flat constancy]\label{lem:flatconst}
Let $\nu\preceq\mu_M$ have rank $k$.  If $B,B'$ are independent
$k$-subsets of $M$ with $\cl(B)=\cl(B')$, then $\nu(B)=\nu(B')$; and
$\nu(A)=\infty$ whenever $A$ is dependent in $M$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The support matroid of $\nu$ is an ordinary quotient of $M$, so its
bases are independent in $M$.  For $k>0$ it suffices to compare
$B=A\cup b$ and $B'=A\cup b'$ spanning the same rank $k$ flat.  Choose
one complement $C$ so that both $AbC$ and $Ab'C$ are $M$-bases.  In the
incidence relation for $\nu\preceq\mu_M$ with lower set $A$ and upper
set $A\cup\{b,b'\}\cup C$, the only finite $\mu_M$-terms are the
$b$- and $b'$-terms.  Thus $\nu(Ab)=\nu(Ab')$.
\end{proof}

Write $\bar\nu(X)$ for the common value on independent $k$-sets spanning
the rank $k$ flat $X$, and put $\bar\nu(X)=\infty$ if no basis of $\nu$
spans $X$.

\section*{2. The polar transform}

For a rank $k$ quotient $\nu\preceq\mu_M$ define
\[
 \nu^{\perp_M}(A)=
 \begin{cases}
   \bar\nu\bigl((\cl A)^\perp\bigr),
      & A\text{ independent in }M,\ |A|=r-k,\\
   \infty,&\text{otherwise.}
 \end{cases}                                                 \tag{2}
\]
This is projectively well-defined, and applying it twice recovers the
original flat coordinates.

\begin{lemma}[ordinary polar rank]\label{lem:ordinary}
Let $N$ be an ordinary quotient of $M$ of rank $k$, with rank function
$\rho_N$.  Then
\[
        \rho^\perp(A)=\rk_M(A)-k+\rho_N((\cl A)^\perp)          \tag{3}
\]
is a matroid rank function.  Its bases are precisely the finite support
of (2).  If $N_p$ is a quotient of $N_q$, then
$N_q^{\perp_M}$ is a quotient of $N_p^{\perp_M}$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Since $N$ is a quotient of $M$, $\rho_N$ is constant on $M$-closures:
if $e\in\cl_M(A)$, then the $N$-rank increment from $A$ to $Ae$ is at
most the corresponding $M$-increment, namely $0$.  First restrict (3) to
flats.  For flats $X\le Y$,
\[
\rho^\perp(Y)-\rho^\perp(X)
 =\rk(Y)-\rk(X)-\bigl(\rho_N(X^\perp)-\rho_N(Y^\perp)\bigr)
\]
is between $0$ and $\rk(Y)-\rk(X)$ by the strong-map increment
inequality, so $\rho^\perp$ is monotone with unit increments on flats.
For flats $X,Y$, modularity of $\rk_M$ and the identities
$(X\wedge Y)^\perp=X^\perp\vee Y^\perp$ and
$(X\vee Y)^\perp=X^\perp\wedge Y^\perp$ reduce submodularity of
$\rho^\perp$ to submodularity of $\rho_N$.

For arbitrary subsets, $\rho^\perp(A)=\rho^\perp(\cl_M A)$.  If
$X=\cl A$ and $Y=\cl B$, then
$\cl(A\cap B)\le X\wedge Y$ and $\cl(A\cup B)=X\vee Y$; by monotonicity
on flats, submodularity for $X,Y$ implies submodularity for $A,B$.
The unit-increment property also passes from flats to subsets.  Thus
(3) is a matroid rank function.  An $M$-independent set $A$ of size
$r-k$ has full $\rho^\perp$-rank exactly when
$\rho_N((\cl A)^\perp)=k$, and $M$-dependent sets cannot be bases; this
is precisely the finite support of (2).

If $N_p$ is a quotient of $N_q$, then on every interval the rank
increment of $N_p$ is at most that of $N_q$.  The displayed formula for
increments therefore reverses the inequality for the polar ranks, which
is exactly the strong-map criterion saying that $N_q^{\perp_M}$ is a
quotient of $N_p^{\perp_M}$.
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[elementary opposition]\label{lem:elementary}
Let $p\preceq q\preceq\mu_M$, with $\rk p=d$ and $\rk q=d+1$.  Then
$q^{\perp_M}$ and $p^{\perp_M}$ satisfy all adjacent three-term
relations of ranks $r-d-1$ and $r-d$.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Fix $A$ of size $r-d-2$ and distinct $x,y,z\notin A$.  Put
$U=\cl(A)$, $W=\cl(Axyz)$, and $\delta=\rk W-\rk U$.  If $A$ is
dependent, or if $\delta\le1$, each term contains a dependent set of the
required size and hence is $\infty$.

If $\delta=2$, let $G=W^\perp$ and $H=U^\perp$, of ranks $d$ and
$d+2$.  Whenever the term indexed by $x$ is finite, $Ayz$ spans $W$,
so its $p$-factor is the common value $\bar p(G)$; its $q$-factor is
$\bar q((\cl(Ax))^\perp)$, and similarly for $y,z$.  In the rank-two
interval $[U,W]$, a loop over $U$ makes all terms involving it
infinite, and two parallel elements give equal corresponding
$q$-coordinates.  Thus only the case of three non-parallel points over
$U$ needs proof.  Choose a basis $S$ of $G$, representatives
$a_x,a_y,a_z$ for the three dual rank-one flats over $G$ in $[G,H]$,
and a complement $C$ of $H$ to a basis of $M$.  In the incidence
relation for $q\preceq\mu_M$ with lower set $S$ and upper set
$S\cup\{a_x,a_y,a_z\}\cup C$, the only finite $\mu_M$-terms are those
obtained by deleting one of $a_x,a_y,a_z$.  Hence the three displayed
$q$-coordinates have a non-unique minimum; adding the common
$\bar p(G)$ proves the transformed relation.

If $\delta=3$, then $x,y,z$ form a Boolean frame over $U$.  Let
$G=W^\perp$ and
\[
 P_x=(\cl(Ayz))^\perp,\quad P_y=(\cl(Axz))^\perp,\quad
 P_z=(\cl(Axy))^\perp .
\]
These are the three atoms over $G$ in the dual frame, and
$(\cl(Ax))^\perp=P_y\vee P_z$, with the analogous identities for
$y,z$.  Choose a basis $S$ of $G$ and representatives
$a_e\in P_e\setminus G$.  The adjacent incidence relation for
$p\preceq q$ applied to the lower set $S$ and to
$S\cup\{a_x,a_y,a_z\}$ is exactly the transformed three-term relation,
using flat constancy to identify the displayed coordinates.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}\label{prop:set}
The formula $\nu\mapsto\nu^{\perp_M}$ defines an involution of the set
underlying $\Dr(M)$.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Insert $\nu$ in the complete flag (1).  We prove inductively that
\[
        h_r^{\perp_M}\preceq h_{r-1}^{\perp_M}\preceq\cdots
        \preceq h_0^{\perp_M}=\mu_M .
\]
After harmless scalar normalization, the right endpoint
$h_0^{\perp_M}=\mu_M$ is clear from (2).  If $r\le1$, there is
nothing more to check beyond the automatic rank-zero quotient.  Now assume $r\ge2$.
Suppose $0\le j\le r-2$ and $h_j^{\perp_M}$ has
already been proved valuated.  Since $h_j\preceq h_{j+1}$, Lemma
\ref{lem:ordinary} says that the ordinary support of
$h_{j+1}^{\perp_M}$ is an elementary quotient of the ordinary support of
$h_j^{\perp_M}$; in particular it satisfies the degenerate support
condition (D) relative to $h_j^{\perp_M}$.  Lemma
\ref{lem:elementary} gives the displayed concurrent relations (C) for
the pair $h_{j+1}^{\perp_M},h_j^{\perp_M}$.  Proposition
\ref{prop:elementarytest} then implies
$h_{j+1}^{\perp_M}\preceq h_j^{\perp_M}$.  This proves the induction
down to $h_{r-1}^{\perp_M}$; the last rank-zero quotient
$h_r^{\perp_M}\preceq h_{r-1}^{\perp_M}$ is automatic.  Hence $\nu^{\perp_M}$, one layer of the flag, lies in
$\Dr(M)$.  Involutivity follows from (2) and $(X^\perp)^\perp=X$.
\end{proof}

\section*{3. Order reversal}

\begin{lemma}[circuit minima]\label{lem:circuit}
Let $w\preceq\mu_M$ have rank $k\ge1$, let $A$ be an independent
$(k-1)$-set of $M$, and let $C$ be a circuit of $M/A$ with $|C|\ge2$.
Then $\min_{i\in C}w(A\cup i)$ is attained at least twice.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Let $F=\cl(A\cup C)$.  For every $i\in C$, $A\cup(C-i)$ is independent
and spans $F$.  Choose one complement $D$ of $F$ to an $M$-basis.  In
the incidence relation for $w\preceq\mu_M$ with lower set $A$ and upper
set $A\cup C\cup D$, the finite $\mu_M$-terms are exactly those indexed
by $i\in C$.
\end{proof}

\begin{theorem}\label{thm:order}
If $p\preceq q\preceq\mu_M$, then
\[
        q^{\perp_M}\preceq p^{\perp_M}.
\]
\end{theorem}

\begin{proof}
Let $\rk p=a$ and $\rk q=b$.  If $a=b$, the strong-map inequalities applied to
$\emptyset\subseteq A$ and to $A\subseteq E$ force the two ordinary
support rank functions to be equal.  Then $p$ and $q$ are projectively
equal: the equal-rank incidence relation on adjacent bases shows
$p(B)-q(B)$ is constant on the common basis graph.  If $a=0$, then $p^{\perp_M}=\mu_M$, so the
assertion is $q^{\perp_M}\preceq\mu_M$; if $b=r$, it is the vacuous
rank-zero lower quotient.  Hence assume $0<a<b<r$.

By Proposition \ref{prop:set} the polars are valuated matroids, and by
Lemma \ref{lem:ordinary} their ordinary supports are in quotient order.
By Criterion \ref{crit:local} it remains to prove nested incidence.
Fix $A\subseteq T$ with
$|A|=r-b-1$, $|T|=r-a+1$, set $B=T\setminus A$, and
$m=|B|=b-a+2$.  If $A$ is dependent all terms are $\infty$.  Otherwise
put $U=\cl(A)$, $W=\cl(T)$, and $\delta=\rk W-\rk U$.  A finite term
forces $T-i$ to be independent, hence $\delta\ge m-1$; since
$\delta\le m$, only two cases remain.

If $\delta=m$, then $T$ is independent over $U$.  Let $G=W^\perp$ and
$H=U^\perp$, of ranks $a-1$ and $b+1$.  The interval generated by $B$
is Boolean; after applying $^\perp$, the flats
$X_i=(\cl(T-i))^\perp$ are the atoms over $G$, and
$Y_i=(\cl(Ai))^\perp$ is the join of all $X_j$ with $j\ne i$.  Choose a
basis $S$ of $G$ and representatives $x_i\in X_i\setminus G$.  The
nested incidence relation for $p\preceq q$ with lower set $S$ and upper
set $S\cup\{x_i:i\in B\}$ is exactly the desired transformed relation.

If $\delta=m-1$, then $B$ has nullity one in $M/A$, hence a unique
circuit $C$.  A loop circuit gives only $\infty$ terms.  Otherwise, for
$i\notin C$ the set $T-i$ is dependent; for $i\in C$ it is independent
and spans $W$, so the $p^{\perp_M}(T-i)$ factor is the common value
$\bar p(W^\perp)$.  Lemma \ref{lem:circuit}, applied to
$w=q^{\perp_M}$ and the circuit $C$ of $M/A$, gives two minima among
the remaining $q^{\perp_M}(Ai)$ terms.  This proves nested incidence.
\end{proof}

\section*{4. Agreement with flats}

Let $F$ be a flat of rank $f$, and let $q_F$ be the trivial rank
$r-f$ valuated matroid of $M/F\oplus U_{0,F}$.  For an independent
$(r-f)$-set $B$ with $Z=\cl(B)$,
\[
        q_F(B)<\infty\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad Z\vee F=\hat1.
\]
For an independent $f$-set $A$ with $Y=\cl(A)$, formula (2) gives
\[
 q_F^{\perp_M}(A)<\infty
 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad Y^\perp\vee F=\hat1 .
\]
By modularity, since $\rk(Y^\perp)=r-f$ and $\rk F=f$, this is
equivalent to $Y^\perp\wedge F=\hat0$.  Applying the anti-automorphism
gives $Y\vee F^\perp=\hat1$, the basis condition for
$M/F^\perp\oplus U_{0,F^\perp}$.  The finite values are zero on both
sides.  Thus the polar transform sends the flat $F$ to $F^\perp$.

Combining Proposition \ref{prop:set}, Theorem \ref{thm:order}, and this
flat computation, the map
\[
        \Phi([\nu])=[\nu^{\perp_M}]
\]
is an order-reversing involution of $\Dr(M)$ extending the given
order-reversing involution of $\cL(M)$.
